summary.title: Port lexical.verbosity to Lexicon view; retire kernel
summary.what: Adds Lexicon.named_entities() yielding NamedEntity records (callables + class-like scopes, token-split) and a static Lexicon.split_tokens helper. Creates slop.lexicon.records module with NamedEntity frozen dataclass. Rewrites run_verbosity to consume Lexicon (was kernel-direct via legacy_v2_shim). Drops the shim. Inlines _CLASS_NODES / _LANG_GLOBS / _extract_type_name into _lexical/hammers.py until hammers ports. Deletes _lexical/verbosity.py (193 lines). Full suite 522 passed + 8 xfailed.
summary.why: First lexical-suite port. Establishes the per-rule substrate pattern: each lexical rule gets one Lexicon view method (or reuses one), the rule layer collapses to threshold-application. Sets up the shared NamedEntity primitive that stutter, cowards, hammers, tautology will all consume next.

decisions.chose: Lexicon.named_entities() yields token-split records, not bare names
decisions.rationale: Every entity-name rule (verbosity, stutter, cowards, hammers, tautology) needs the snake/Camel-split tokens, not just the raw identifier. Pre-splitting once on the view eliminates per-rule duplication of split_identifier calls. NamedEntity record carries tokens as the canonical primitive.

decisions.chose: Lexicon.split_tokens as a static method (no view state needed)
decisions.rationale: The split logic is grammar-agnostic — operates on the identifier string alone. Exposing as a staticmethod lets rule code call Lexicon.split_tokens(text) without an instance, and avoids the awkward import-from-_lexical-internals pattern.

Hammers still imports those from _lexical.verbosity. The honest minimum-scope fix is to inline them into hammers (the one remaining consumer); they'll retire with hammers in the next intent. Cleaner than leaving a verbosity.py shell or restructuring scope.

decisions.chose: kind="function" / "class" as bare strings, not enums
decisions.rationale: Rule consumers only need to discriminate between the two values. Adding an EntityKind enum would over-engineer the surface; bare strings are explicit and easy to filter on.

review_notes: Lexicon.named_entities() filters by Lexicon's existing where/under predicates, so language-scoped queries (e.g. Python-only) work out of the box.
NamedEntity.file is absolute by design. The rule converts to relative against slop_config.root when displaying. Matches the convention used by other view records.

Six lexical kernels remain (stutter, cowards, hammers, tautology, slackers, confusion); two are already substrate-native (sprawl, imposters) and just need _v2 suffix dropped. After this intent: 6 shimmed lexical rules remain.

architectural_claims: Lexicon now exposes NamedEntity-yielding view method — the canonical primitive for entity-name rules.
Lexicon.split_tokens is the substrate-aligned home for identifier token splitting; rules no longer import from slop._lexical._naming.
Lexicon's existing where/under filters compose naturally with named_entities for language-scoped or path-scoped name queries.
behavioral_changes: run_verbosity signature changes from (root, rc, sc) to (lexicon, rc, sc).
Rule summary loses 'items_checked' and 'files_searched' keys; new key 'entities_analyzed' replaces them.
Class scopes that emit as ScopeKind.CLASS / INTERFACE / STRUCT / TRAIT all participate (substrate-aligned set; legacy kernel had a per-grammar _CLASS_NODES table).
migration_notes: Custom code calling verbosity_kernel directly will break. Use Tree.lexicon.named_entities() then filter / threshold per the rule's logic.
Test code asserting result.summary['items_checked'] should switch to 'entities_analyzed'.
